The real quarterly expiration is based on futures (third Friday of March, June, September, and December).  Month end is month end.

** Review each Weekly Post every Monday (https://stochvoltrader.substack.com/s/market-analysis?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

comment.body:
The problem with your and othercomments in general is that you put a one-dimensional framing on a multidimensional problem. Short volatility for example can have a multitude of expressions (single stock volatility, index volatility, VIX), through a multitude of instruments or option structures/baskets. Any options trader worth their salt shorts overpriced volatility, not just volatility. So there is a lot more thought and analysis behind a mere "short volatility". So unless you don't know what she was shorting and you don't explain what you are watching to come to your conclusion your questions will remain unanswered. And like stated before, things change. If a big position is unwound or bought, these flows will change the profile and might trump the premarket analysis.
And maybe a more general note: We all signed up for the course in racing a Formula 1 car and yet people keep asking if they should go left or right at the next curve. Or keep asking what this steering wheel is everybody talks about. Everybody here wants up or down, bull or bear, after this OpEX this, after that OpEx that. Fact is nobody knows and even the insiders can be run over. So her analysis is like a torch in a dark basement wherethe furniture is constantly rearranged. If you don't have your own metrics and sensors to gauge you will lack conviction in what you do and then you will sell out of positions or buy positions because you interpreted something Alma said in this way. Maybe you make money, maybe you don't. But if you don't have a proper framework to apply Almas knowledge you will continue to get random results. A lot of her commentary will only make sense ifyou know and understand a fair amount of markets, instruments, market microstructure. And the nuances. She provides a fair amount of guidance in the longer term and provides also some valuable indicators. But she also tells the technical stuff and the nuances and this is where most people get lost. But this is exactly the stuff I pay for. Because it takes years to learn this and to get to that level. And don’t get me wrong, I have a lot of sympathy for the fact that all of this can be confusing. It also took me years to understand all of this.

comment.body: Well, the thing is, if you shorted volatility on Thursday, as I've said many times, in order to be in alignment with her analysis spot would have needed to continue to decline. However, as I stated, she actually ended up being correct on shorting volatility even though spot ripped by 200 points. So for her to say on Thursday "if market rips, that's spot up/vol up + low liquidity" and then to take credit for shorting volatility even though it went contradictory to her own analysis, I think that deserves criticism. This kind of instance is what criticism is for. Moreover, she did not take responsibility or account for volatility acting in the exact opposite way of what she said it would on Thursday. comment.body: That's a fair response and I definitely understand the multitude of variables that are in play when Alma writes her analysis. However, given her analysis, it would seem she put a one dimensional frame on volatility saying spot up/vol up and spot down/vol down under 5565. Now if it was simply explained as "in this environment, I am shorting vol because it is overpriced and is likely to decrease, irregardless of where spot trades here" then that would make a lot more sense as to why she would be shorting vol. But she said very directly, numerous times last Thursday, "spot up-vol up" and "if market rips, that's spot up/volup + low liquidity". So why would you short vol if you were expecting the spot to rally into March OPEX? And why would she also take out call options last Thursday along with shorting vol when those trades go against "spot up-vol up", especially with where spot closed on Thursday (near 5510)? Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?

comment.body:
Also, we all signed up for a course to increase the effectiveness of our trading by being more profitable. Her analysis is the finished product of where we hope to be after learning this course. However, if there is an instance where that analysis goes awry, then it’s a good learning experience to understand what went wrong or what was misunderstood. You could have all the best analysis there is, but if you don’t know how to implement it effectively into a trading strategy, then it’s good for nothing. That is just as important as the analysis itself.

So really, the race track is the market, the race car is her analysis, and how to drive the race car is how to apply this analysis into a successful trading strategy, imho.

comment.body:
The problem with your and othercomments in general is that you put a one-dimensional framing on a multidimensional problem. Short volatility for example can have a multitude of expressions (single stock volatility, index volatility, VIX), through a multitude of instruments or option structures/baskets. Any options trader worth their salt shorts overpriced volatility, not just volatility. So there is a lot more thought and analysis behind a mere "short volatility". So unless you don't know what she was shorting and you don't explain what you are watching to come to your conclusion your questions will remain unanswered. And like stated before, things change. If a big position is unwound or bought, these flows will change the profile and might trump the premarket analysis.
And maybe a more general note: We all signed up for the course in racing a Formula 1 car and yet people keep asking if they should go left or right at the next curve. Or keep asking what this steering wheel is everybody talks about. Everybody here wants up or down, bull or bear, after this OpEX this, after that OpEx that. Fact is nobody knows and even the insiders can be run over. So her analysis is like a torch in a dark basement wherethe furniture is constantly rearranged. If you don't have your own metrics and sensors to gauge you will lack conviction in what you do and then you will sell out of positions or buy positions because you interpreted something Alma said in this way. Maybe you make money, maybe you don't. But if you don't have a proper framework to apply Almas knowledge you will continue to get random results. A lot of her commentary will only make sense ifyou know and understand a fair amount of markets, instruments, market microstructure. And the nuances. She provides a fair amount of guidance in the longer term and provides also some valuable indicators. But she also tells the technical stuff and the nuances and this is where most people get lost. But this is exactly the stuff I pay for. Because it takes years to learn this and to get to that level. And don’t get me wrong, I have a lot of sympathy for the fact that all of this can be confusing. It also took me years to understand all of this.

comment.body: (Reposting this as for some reason it didn't reply to your comment): That's a fair response and I definitely understand the multitude of variables that are in play when Alma writes her analysis. However, given her analysis, it would seem she put a one dimensional frame on volatility saying spot up/vol up and spot down/vol down under 5565. Now if it was simply explained as "in this environment, I am shorting vol because it is overpriced and is likely to decrease, irregardless of where spot trades here" then that would make a lot more sense as to why she would be shorting vol. But she said very directly, numerous times last Thursday, "spot up-vol up" and "if market rips, that's spot up/volup + low liquidity". So why would you short vol if you were expecting the spot to rally into March OPEX? And why would she also take out call options last Thursday along with shorting vol when those trades go against "spot up-vol up", especially with where spot closed on Thursday (near 5510)? Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?

I was wondering when cavalry would arrive to attack the left flank. Always on time 0Mik.
So her writing last Thursday about a JPM collar trade that was due to expire in 2.5 weeks by potentially dragging price down in a spot down/vol down environment towards 4700 is intraday vol noise? Sssuuurrreee. It may have been in the “intraday post” but just because it is doesn’t mean she wasn’t giving analysis that had implications on a larger time frame

I’m dropping quotes from the actual post this entire time. Are you actually being honest about differentiating what she’s writing herself? Doesn’t seem like it. And I find it humorous that the most snarky, defensive person in this group is telling me to stop being snarky.

So she actually took trades that went against her spot decay analysis and her vol decay analysis. As she said spot up, vol up. Give me the quotes she said from last Thursday where she said otherwise. This is getting a bit ridiculous.

comment.body: Yes, of course she oversimplifies things, because that's the only way. I don't want to assume what Alma means and I don't want to speak for her. But I can tell you how I read and understand these posts: when I read "short volatility" then I would see this as a signal to look if I can find some pricey vol and structure a trade around it. And the trade will depend on my assessment of where the market is going. Because short vol can mean short put or short call (or more complex of course). So when she writes short vol I understand that the edge is in the volatility short, not in the market direction call. That does not mean that the market will not move and you can even lose on the delta. But depending on the structure the effect from your vega will/should trump the effect from your delta. That's all I want to read into a statement like this. I also don't want to comment on her trades because I don't really follow them too close and also don't I don't know and what she is trading . But I can give you an example of how you could look at this whole spot up/vol up statement. Spot up / vol up in this context just gives you the market dynamics you could expect and that you should consider in your trade structure. Like telling your friend to drive slow because there is ice on the street. That does not mean you cannot drive (or short vol), but you need to consider the environment before you get into the car. So this statement reduces a three-dimensional volatility surface (time, strike, IV) to a single observation. So we could assume a parallel shift up, that means IV rises by the same amount on all strikes and expiries (your two-dimensional smile shifts up by the same distance everywhere). In practice that does not happen like this. Skew rises or falls, 3d vol is bid more than 3M vol, etc... you catch my drift.  So there is a multitude of possibilities how the surface can move and for you to structure a trade around it. So let's just make an example up. We just sold off for a few days and therefore don't expect much more short term downside generally over the next week or two. So we might have a neutral to slightly bullish bias. Puts 2 weeks out seem to be rich compared to your forecast of declining realized vol. So in the simplest example I could short some SPX puts with a high vega (let's say slightly ITM) and scalp my gamma (ie delta hedge). We can also up this one notch. Maybe AAPL IV is rich compared to index, so I might go short AAPL puts instead (if I also think that AAPL will not sell of further). Once you have that trade on, the real fun starts.  The basic idea behind short vol is that you want to isolate your vegas from the rest of the Pnl. So practically speaking you short volatility and try to be as (delta) neutral as possible. So now let's say the spot moves against you and your deltas pick up (you are long delta and have negative gamma). So you need to reduce your long delta exposure. So you could just sell AAPL stock until your deltas are back in line with your risk appetite. If we are in a mean reversion environment, that means you will usually buy the top and short the hole. So with that in mind maybe you might give your deltas more leeway and only hedge at a critical point. Now let's turn this around. We are generally in a spot up/ vol up environment. So let's say spot goes in our favor and people start buying 0, 1, 2DTEs (let's assume they exist on AAPL). So short end vol is bid and you start losing on your vega a little bit. The 2W IV moves a little as well, but not as much. So you still decide to reduce your vega exposure before it explodes in your face. So you might opt to long a call. Since you don't want to hedge your vega completely you might opt for a call a little further out (maybe 2W as well) and OTM. So if the market rallies against your expectation the vanna will pick up and compensate (some of) the short vega. But let's suppose instead of AAPL you realize that you can get cheaper vegas in GOOGL, where vol seems underpriced in relation to the market. So you might put on a relative value trade and buy a slightly OTM call on GOOGL 2 weeks out. There is a lot of discretion and in the end all of this is more an art than a science and like everything in trading comes down to risk aversion, personal preference, etc.

comment.body: That's a fair response and I definitely understand the multitude of variables that are in play when Alma writes her analysis. However, given her analysis, it would seem she put a one dimensional frame on volatility saying spot up/vol up and spot down/vol down under 5565. Now if it was simply explained as "in this environment, I am shorting vol because it is overpriced and is likely to decrease, irregardless of where spot trades here" then that would make a lot more sense as to why she would be shorting vol. But she said very directly, numerous times last Thursday, "spot up-vol up" and "if market rips, that's spot up/volup + low liquidity". So why would you short vol if you were expecting the spot to rally into March OPEX? And why would she also take out call options last Thursday along with shorting vol when those trades go against "spot up-vol up", especially with where spot closed on Thursday (near 5510)? Thursday was a big turning point for this 200 point rally and her short vol trade, and if you were to take her at her analysis at face value, then you'd be behind the 8-ball. Unless you went against it and still won anyways. Let me ask you something... which expression of volatility do you think Alma was referencing last Thursday?

comment.body: I think it all comes down if you buy into Almas thesis of a managed decline in relation to the ongoing Jeddah negotiations. Depending how big the Saudi buyin will be it will take a few days to weeks to work into those positions. If the baseline given by Alma (decline into Q2 and then somewhere in Q2/Q3 the ceasefire event is the turning point then that does not leave too much time to walk the market lower another 5-15% (just guessing here) without crashing it. So in that regard I don't think it's likely we rally into mid April. With that thesis a lot of your stated indicators also lose value because it's not a normal market behavior / price discovery. On the other hand if you don't believe in the thesis then your indications have a much higher value and this might actually happen.

comment.body: Yes, of course she oversimplifies things, because that's the only way. I don't want to assume what Alma means and I don't want to speak for her. But I can tell you how I read and understand these posts: when I read "short volatility" then I would see this as a signal to look if I can find some pricey vol and structure a trade around it. And the trade will depend on my assessment of where the market is going. Because short vol can mean short put or short call (or more complex of course). So when she writes short vol I understand that the edge is in the volatility short, not in the market direction call. That does not mean that the market will not move and you can even lose on the delta. But depending on the structure the effect from your vega will/should trump the effect from your delta. That's all I want to read into a statement like this. I also don't want to comment on her trades because I don't really follow them too close and also don't I don't know and what she is trading . But I can give you an example of how you could look at this whole spot up/vol up statement. Spot up / vol up in this context just gives you the market dynamics you could expect and that you should consider in your trade structure. Like telling your friend to drive slow because there is ice on the street. That does not mean you cannot drive (or short vol), but you need to consider the environment before you get into the car. So this statement reduces a three-dimensional volatility surface (time, strike, IV) to a single observation. So we could assume a parallel shift up, that means IV rises by the same amount on all strikes and expiries (your two-dimensional smile shifts up by the same distance everywhere). In practice that does not happen like this. Skew rises or falls, 3d vol is bid more than 3M vol, etc... you catch my drift.  So there is a multitude of possibilities how the surface can move and for you to structure a trade around it. So let's just make an example up. We just sold off for a few days and therefore don't expect much more short term downside generally over the next week or two. So we might have a neutral to slightly bullish bias. Puts 2 weeks out seem to be rich compared to your forecast of declining realized vol. So in the simplest example I could short some SPX puts with a high vega (let's say slightly ITM) and scalp my gamma (ie delta hedge). We can also up this one notch. Maybe AAPL IV is rich compared to index, so I might go short AAPL puts instead (if I also think that AAPL will not sell of further). Once you have that trade on, the real fun starts.  The basic idea behind short vol is that you want to isolate your vegas from the rest of the Pnl. So practically speaking you short volatility and try to be as (delta) neutral as possible. So now let's say the spot moves against you and your deltas pick up (you are long delta and have negative gamma). So you need to reduce your long delta exposure. So you could just sell AAPL stock until your deltas are back in line with your risk appetite. If we are in a mean reversion environment, that means you will usually buy the top and short the hole. So with that in mind maybe you might give your deltas more leeway and only hedge at a critical point. Now let's turn this around. We are generally in a spot up/ vol up environment. So let's say spot goes in our favor and people start buying 0, 1, 2DTEs (let's assume they exist on AAPL). So short end vol is bid and you start losing on your vega a little bit. The 2W IV moves a little as well, but not as much. So you still decide to reduce your vega exposure before it explodes in your face. So you might opt to long a call. Since you don't want to hedge your vega completely you might opt for a call a little further out (maybe 2W as well) and OTM. So if the market rallies against your expectation the vanna will pick up and compensate (some of) the short vega. But let's suppose instead of AAPL you realize that you can get cheaper vegas in GOOGL, where vol seems underpriced in relation to the market. So you might put on a relative value trade and buy a slightly OTM call on GOOGL 2 weeks out. There is a lot of discretion and in the end all of this is more an art than a science and like everything in trading comes down to risk aversion, personal preference, etc.

comment.body:
There’s definitely meaningful increase in skew so idk what data you’re looking at there. 

We’ve had dark pool trades all this week and you know what that reminds me of? When Alma said they need more liquidity to bring it down further. Tuesday we had multi-billion dollar downside directional trades for 6/20 for selling calls and buying puts, some were part of spreads, but the spread together were selling calls and buying puts and there was still a very large chunk of just naked shorting essentially. 

I look at frequency and modulation techniques at mathematically extracting dominant wavelengths from markets and they suggest too we’re close to a relatively larger bottom but not quite there yet. That’s what I’m seeing and the perspective I have, and it doesn’t help we can’t get over 5700 which has been the MM GEX flip for a while now. Not great signs, 

I think the only thing holding this market up is the supportive flows from vanna and charm Alma has talked  about for a supportive OPEX week

My $0.02
